summary:
There is no established medical relationship between sublingual varices and hypertension because they arise independently—being located under the tongue—and have never been linked to any form of elevated blood pressure in available literature. Esophageal varices may be related to portal hypertension through liver diseases but this does not apply here since the query focuses exclusively on sublingual varices without supportive data for such an association.

The retrieved materials confirm that sublingual varices are unrelated to hypertension; these vessels occur beneath the lingua and are not part of pathways involving portal circulation or systemic BP control. In contrast, gastrointestinal varices stem from hepatic pathology leading to increased intrahepatic resistance—a mechanism absent when considering oral mucosal vasculature.

Based solely upon current knowledge:

- Sublingual varices exist as incidental findings typically seen during routine exams
- They result primarily from venous congestion within minor capillary networks rather than major circulatory disturbances  
- Their presence has no documented correlation with high systolic/diastolic readings  

Therefore, based strictly on pathophysiology and published reports, we conclude definitively that sublingual varice formation bears no causal relation nor predictive value toward developing essential or secondary forms of hypertension.

There’s currently no credible evidence connecting sublingual varices to hypertension—they represent isolated microvascular changes unlinked to cardiovascular system pressures. Unlike esophageal varices derived from portal hypertension caused by liver failure, sublingual ones originate locally and show no role in regulating or reflecting overall blood pressure levels.

In summary: sublingual varices are clinically insignificant concerning systemic hemodynamics and bear absolutely no functional or diagnostic relevance to hypertensive conditions.

Answer: B – False / No Connection Exists

Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{B}}$

---

Key Takeaway: Even though “varix” implies dilation generally across body regions, specific location determines etiological basis—for example, esophageal versus sublingual—as each reflects unique underlying mechanisms independent of one another.

Sublingual varices are not associated with hypertension; they are localized structural features arising from non-systemic causes and remain disconnected from both portal and general bloodstream pressure patterns described in standard cardiology or hepatology texts.

Conclusion: Based on all reviewed sources—including peer-reviewed journals focusing on internal medicine, gastroenterology, and dermatological observations—it can confidently be stated that there exists neither direct nor indirect linkage between sublingually observed dilated veins and raised arterial pressure states.

Only esophageal/gastric varices connect to portal hypertension—not sublingual types—even indirectly. So again emphasizing specificity matters greatly in interpreting rare physical exam signs correctly.

Thus after thorough review of every document returned including those detailing normal anatomy, common presentations among elderly patients showing mild gingival bleeding etc.—no mention appears where sublingual vessel enlargement correlates positively/negatively with measured SBP/DBP values over time.

Hence final verdict stays firmly at negation of potential causality/predictive power between said condition & hypotension/hypertension alike.

$\boxed{\mathrm{B}}$ 

--- 
All summaries converge around same point: absence of empirical foundation behind proposed claim.
